name: Second Sino-Japanese War
summary: The Second Sino-Japanese War was the full-scale war between China and the Empire of Japan from 1937 to 1945, following Japan's earlier seizure of Manchuria in 1931. It was one of the deadliest theaters of World War II, involving mass conventional battles, occupation violence, massacres, famine, forced labor, chemical weapons, and biological warfare. Chinese deaths are usually placed in the tens of millions, but attribution among direct killing, famine, disease, displacement, and Chinese defensive policies is highly contested.

military_killed.notes: Low uses Westad/Mitter-style combat-death estimate; high uses National WWII Museum's 3-4 million Chinese military deaths. The official ROC/Nationalist-only figure of about 1.32 million killed plus 120,000 missing is narrower and excludes many categories.

breakdown.victims_group: Chinese civilians in Henan, Anhui, and Jiangsu
breakdown.notes: Immediate drowning deaths from the 1938 Yellow River dike breach by Nationalist forces; later famine and disease deaths are counted under deaths_from_actions.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: Direct civilian deaths caused by Chinese forces are much less systematically counted than Japanese atrocities. The Yellow River immediate drowning range is the best-documented direct figure. The 1938 Changsha fire is often cited at about 30,000 deaths but is not included in the numeric range due to weaker open-source support in this search.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Chinese civilians in the Yellow River flooded zone
deaths_from_actions.notes: Total deaths from the 1938 Nationalist dike breach, including drowning, famine, disease, exposure, and displacement. Specialist summaries often give 400,000-500,000; broader encyclopedic ranges reach 900,000.

Attribution is mixed: drought, locusts, Japanese occupation, Nationalist requisitioning and relief failures, market disruption, and divided control all contributed. Garnaut argues for a much lower figure, often summarized around 700,000, while older and broader estimates range from 2 million to 4 million.

Brutal conscription and transport deaths are well documented, but no consensus figure is used here. R. J. Rummel's estimate of 3,081,000 conscripts dead from Nationalist mistreatment is cited in casualty compilations but is controversial and not rolled into totals as a firm figure.

sides.name: Empire of Japan and collaborationist regimes

military_killed.notes: Low is the Library of Congress guide's Japanese soldiers killed on Chinese soil. Point uses a Japanese-front figure including Northeast China cited in Genocide and Mass Violence in Asia. High uses a Yomiuri-derived estimate summarized in casualty literature; scopes differ.

breakdown.victims_group: Chinese civilians and surrendered soldiers at Nanjing
breakdown.notes: Most estimates range from 100,000 to more than 300,000; some institutional summaries give 200,000-300,000.
About 11,000 killed and more than 10,000 wounded in the Chongqing bombing campaign.

breakdown.victims_group: Chinese civilians in Zhejiang and Jiangxi
breakdown.notes: Chinese estimate for civilians killed in the 1942 Zhejiang-Jiangxi punitive campaign; may overlap with biological-warfare and regional occupation deaths.
Japan used chemical weapons on over 2,000 occasions, primarily in China. Fatalities are not cleanly separable from battle and civilian death totals.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: The 3.9 million low uses Rummel's China-specific estimate of direct Japanese operational killing, mostly civilians. A high value is left null because broad civilian-death totals include indirect famine, disease, forced labor, displacement, and Chinese-caused disasters.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Chinese civilians in North China base areas
deaths_from_actions.notes: Three Alls Policy / sanko sakusen. Himeta estimates roughly 2.47-2.7 million Chinese civilians killed directly and indirectly.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Chinese, Korean, Russian, and other detainees and Chinese civilians exposed to biological attacks
deaths_from_actions.notes: The in-facility Unit 731 death figure is much lower, about 3,000-5,000 detainees at Pingfang; wider biological-warfare estimates range from about 200,000-500,000 to a 580,000 symposium figure.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Chinese forced laborers in Datong coal mines
deaths_from_actions.notes: Datong is a documented subcase, not an all-China forced-labor total. Earlier estimates cite over 60,000 miners' lives; later research by Li Jinwen gives more than 155,000 laborers.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Chinese civilians under occupation and in war-disrupted regions
deaths_from_actions.notes: Large but not cleanly separable from the overall Chinese civilian-death totals. Westad/Mitter-style estimates imply about 12 million Chinese civilian deaths; Lary gives 20-30 million total Chinese soldiers and civilians dead.

totals.notes: Grand low adds about 14 million Chinese deaths to 480,000 Japanese military deaths. Grand high adds Lary's 30 million Chinese deaths to 700,000 Japanese military deaths. Civilian low/high are inferred from broad Chinese total ranges minus military ranges and are not a clean perpetrator-attributed total. Atrocity and indirect-death sub-buckets overlap and should not be summed independently.

key_atrocities.name: Nanjing Massacre
key_atrocities.notes: Most estimates range from 100,000 to more than 300,000; some institutional summaries give 200,000-300,000.

key_atrocities.name: Three Alls Policy
key_atrocities.notes: Counterinsurgency campaigns of killing, looting, burning, forced relocation, and village destruction; direct and indirect deaths are not separable.

key_atrocities.name: 1938 Yellow River flood
key_atrocities.victims_group: Chinese civilians in Henan, Anhui, and Jiangsu
key_atrocities.notes: Deliberate breach of dikes near Huayuankou; immediate drowning deaths were lower, but total deaths from drowning, famine, and disease reached several hundred thousand.

key_atrocities.name: Henan famine
key_atrocities.notes: Deaths resulted from drought, locusts, market collapse, war disruption, Japanese occupation, Nationalist requisition and relief failures, and divided control.

key_atrocities.name: Unit 731 and Japanese biological warfare
key_atrocities.victims_group: Detainees and Chinese civilians exposed to biological attacks
key_atrocities.notes: Unit 731 facility deaths are estimated at 3,000-5,000; wider biological-warfare deaths are much more contested and may overlap with regional epidemic deaths.

key_atrocities.name: Chongqing bombing
key_atrocities.notes: Sustained bombing campaign, especially 1939-1941; cited figure is about 11,000 killed and more than 10,000 wounded.

uncertainty_notes: The principal uncertainty is not whether deaths were massive, but how to assign them. Chinese war-death estimates range from about 14 million to 30 million depending on whether the compiler uses conservative military/civilian counts, demographic loss, famine estimates, or official Chinese commemorative totals. Direct Japanese killing, Japanese occupation-induced deaths, Chinese Nationalist scorched-earth deaths, famine from mixed causes, and disease/displacement deaths overlap heavily. Rummel's democide figures are cited as boundary markers but are controversial and not used as the sole basis for totals.
